title: How to reduce hallucination: grounding, RAG, and guardrails

questions.question: What does RAG stand for, and what problem does it solve?
options: Retrieval-Augmented Generation, it gives the model real source material to work from instead of relying on training memory
Randomness-Adjusted Generation, it lowers temperature to reduce hallucination
Rule-Augmented Generation, it adds a rule layer on top of the model's output
Retrieval-Assisted Grounding, it connects the model to a live internet search
questions.explanation: RAG retrieves relevant documents and includes them in the prompt, so the model generates answers based on real, current source material rather than from pattern-matching in training data alone. It's the single most impactful technique for reducing hallucination.

questions.question: Which system prompt instruction would most directly reduce Karel's risk of falsely confirming a fraud report was filed?
options: 'Always be polite and empathetic when speaking to customers.'
'Never confirm that an action has been completed unless you have received a successful response from the tool.'
'Do not discuss competitor banks or financial products.'
'Respond in the customer's preferred language if possible.'
questions.explanation: This instruction directly addresses the hallucinated-action failure mode. It tells Karel to wait for tool confirmation before declaring success, closing the gap between 'Karel generated a confirmation' and 'the action was actually performed'.

questions.question: Karel successfully files a fraud report via the tool API. Before showing the customer a confirmation message, what should the system do?
options: Nothing, if Karel said it worked, it worked
Ask Karel to double-check by calling the tool a second time
Check that the tool returned a success status before displaying the confirmation
Show the customer a disclaimer that the report may or may not have been filed
questions.explanation: Output validation means checking the tool's actual response before surfacing anything to the user. If the tool returned an error, Karel's confirmation message should be blocked. This is a separate guardrail layer independent of what Karel says.
